name: John Edwards
charges_filed: Six counts: conspiracy to violate federal campaign finance laws, receiving illegal campaign contributions, concealing illegal donations (18 U.S.C. §371; 52 U.S.C. §30116)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of North Carolina (Greensboro)
resolution_details: Jury acquitted Edwards on Count 3 (receiving illegal campaign contributions). Jury deadlocked on the remaining five counts; judge declared a mistrial. The DOJ declined to retry the case. Edwards faced allegations that nearly $1 million from donors was funneled to conceal his affair with Rielle Hunter during his 2008 presidential run.
source1_description: DOJ Press Release: John Edwards Indictment (June 3, 2011)
source2_description: NPR: Jury Acquits Edwards On 1 Count, Mistrial On Others (June 1, 2012)
notes: Edwards admitted to moral failing but consistently denied criminal liability. The legal theory — that donor payments to a mistress constituted campaign contributions — was novel and contested. DOJ did not seek retrial.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Ted Stevens
role_title: U.S. Senator (R-AK); Senate President Pro Tempore (2003–2007)
charges_filed: Seven counts of making false statements on Senate financial disclosure forms (18 U.S.C. §1001) — specifically failing to report gifts and home renovations valued at over $250,000 from VECO Corporation and its CEO
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Washington, D.C.)
resolution_details: Stevens was convicted on all seven counts in October 2008, one week before losing his Senate re-election bid. In April 2009, U.S. District Judge Emmet G. Sullivan vacated the conviction and dismissed all charges with prejudice after the DOJ (under AG Eric Holder) disclosed that prosecutors had committed egregious misconduct — including withholding exculpatory evidence, introducing false business records, and concealing the grand jury testimony of key witnesses. Judge Sullivan appointed a special prosecutor to investigate the DOJ attorneys. Stevens died in a plane crash in August 2010 without his reputation restored.
source1_description: NACDL: Prosecution of Senator Ted Stevens — Case Summary and Special Prosecutor Report
source2_description: CNN: Sen. Ted Stevens' conviction set aside (April 7, 2009)
notes: Judge Sullivan stated he had 'never seen anything approaching the mishandling and misconduct' seen in this case. The case is widely cited as a landmark example of prosecutorial misconduct. Stevens lost his seat largely because of the (vacated) conviction.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Tom DeLay
charges_filed: Money laundering (two counts) and conspiracy to commit money laundering — related to alleged illegal funneling of $190,000 in corporate funds through the Republican National State Elections Committee to Texas state legislative candidates in 2002 (Texas Penal Code §34.02)
jurisdiction: Travis County District Court, Texas; appeal to Texas Third Court of Appeals; final review by Texas Court of Criminal Appeals
resolution_details: DeLay was convicted in November 2010 and sentenced to three years in prison. In September 2013, the Texas Third Court of Appeals overturned both convictions 2-1, holding that the evidence was legally insufficient to prove money laundering — specifically that the State failed to prove the funds were 'proceeds of criminal activity.' On October 1, 2014, the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals affirmed that decision 8-1, permanently exonerating DeLay. He never served prison time due to appellate stays.
source1_description: Texas Tribune: Court Backs Decision Reversing DeLay's Convictions (October 1, 2014)
source2_description: NPR: Tom DeLay's Conviction Overturned On Appeal (September 19, 2013)
notes: DeLay resigned from Congress in 2006 during the investigation. The Texas CCA found the State had failed to prove 'the applicable culpable mental states' required for the statute.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Rick Perry
charges_filed: Count I: Abuse of official capacity (1st-degree felony) — alleged misuse of $7.5M in appropriated state funds; Count II: Coercion of a public servant (3rd-degree felony) — threatening to veto funds unless Travis County DA Rosemary Lehmberg resigned
jurisdiction: Travis County, Texas; Texas Third Court of Appeals; Texas Court of Criminal Appeals
resolution_details: Indicted on August 15, 2014. Count II (coercion) was dismissed by the Texas Third Court of Appeals on July 24, 2015, as an unconstitutional restriction on the governor's First Amendment rights. Count I (abuse of capacity) was dismissed by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals on February 24, 2016, on separation-of-powers grounds — the court held that prosecuting a governor for exercising veto authority was constitutionally impermissible. Perry was the longest-serving governor in Texas history.
source1_description: Texas Tribune: Texas Court of Criminal Appeals Dismisses Rick Perry Indictment (February 24, 2016)
source2_description: BBC News: Rick Perry — Charges against ex-Texas governor dismissed (February 24, 2016)
notes: Perry pleaded not guilty and called the prosecution 'a baseless political attack.' The case was initiated by a nonprofit watchdog group. Perry later served as U.S. Secretary of Energy under President Trump.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Kay Bailey Hutchison
government_body: Texas State Government; United States Senate
charges_filed: Four felony counts: misuse of state employees and state equipment (computers, phones) for personal political advantage during her 1993 U.S. Senate campaign; attempted concealment of evidence
jurisdiction: Travis County District Court, Texas (later moved to Fort Worth / Tarrant County)
resolution_details: The trial judge, John F. Onion Jr., directed a verdict of acquittal just minutes after the jury was sworn in, ruling that prosecutors had failed to produce adequate evidence. The judge refused to admit the prosecution's evidence. Jurors deliberated for approximately eight minutes and unanimously returned not-guilty verdicts on all four felony counts. An earlier indictment had been thrown out when an ineligible grand jury member was discovered. Prosecution was led by Travis County DA Ronnie Earle.
source1_description: New York Times: Fight Over Evidence Results in Acquittal Of Senator in Texas (February 12, 1994)
source2_description: Los Angeles Times: Senator Acquitted of Misusing Old Office (February 12, 1994)
notes: Hutchison had been elected to the U.S. Senate in a 1993 special election during the pendency of the charges. The case was moved from Austin to Fort Worth due to concerns about a politically charged atmosphere. Defense attorney Dick DeGuerin argued there was 'no evidence of any nature' of wrongdoing.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Caspar Weinberger
government_body: U.S. Department of Defense — Reagan Administration Cabinet
charges_filed: Five felony counts: two counts of perjury (lying to Congress about knowledge of arms sales to Iran and diversion of funds to Contras), one count of obstruction of justice, plus additional charges related to withholding notes from the Independent Counsel (18 U.S.C. §§1001, 1505, 1621)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia
resolution_details: Weinberger was indicted on June 16, 1992. His criminal trial was scheduled to begin January 3, 1993. On December 24, 1992, outgoing President George H.W. Bush granted Weinberger a full pardon (along with five other Iran-Contra defendants) before the trial could proceed. Independent Counsel Lawrence Walsh called the pardon a 'cover-up.' The charges were accordingly dismissed. The pardon effectively ended the Iran-Contra prosecutions.
source1_description: Politico: Bush pardons Iran-Contra felons, Dec. 24, 1992
source2_description: New York Times (archived): Bush Pardons 6 in Iran Affair, Aborting a Weinberger Trial
notes: Walsh argued that Bush's pardon, issued just before trial, was itself part of a cover-up. Notes Weinberger had kept in his diary — which he had denied having — contradicted his congressional testimony and were central to the prosecution's case. No conviction was ever entered.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Oliver North
role_title: Lieutenant Colonel, USMC; National Security Council Staff Member
charges_filed: Originally 12 felony counts; proceeded to trial on: destroying government documents, accepting an illegal gratuity (security fence), and aiding and abetting obstruction of Congress (related to Iran-Contra arms deal and Contra funding diversion)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia
resolution_details: North was convicted on three felony counts in May 1989 and sentenced to probation, fines, and community service. The D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals reversed the conviction for destroying documents outright and remanded the other two counts for a hearing on whether witnesses' testimony was tainted by North's immunized congressional testimony. On remand, Independent Counsel Lawrence Walsh concluded he could not establish that any of the 67 witnesses at trial had been unaffected by North's immunized testimony. Walsh moved to dismiss all remaining charges on September 16, 1991, and Judge Gerhard Gesell immediately dismissed the case. North declared himself 'fully, completely exonerated.'
source1_description: New York Times: Judge in Iran-Contra Trial Drops Case Against North After Prosecutor Gives Up (September 17, 1991)
source2_description: Tampa Bay Times: Iran-Contra case against Oliver North is dropped (September 17, 1991)
notes: North had testified before Congress under a grant of immunity, which ultimately made it impossible to use testimony from witnesses who had seen his immunized statements. The Supreme Court declined to reinstate his conviction in May 1991. North later ran unsuccessfully for the U.S. Senate from Virginia (1994) and served as NRA president (2018–2019).
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Andrew Cuomo
government_body: State of New York — Executive Branch
charges_filed: Misdemeanor forcible touching (sex crime) — Albany County criminal complaint filed October 28, 2021, by Albany County Sheriff Craig Apple's office on behalf of accuser Brittany Commisso. Four additional criminal inquiries in other counties were simultaneously pursued.
jurisdiction: Albany City Court, New York; additional jurisdictions including Oswego County
resolution_details: On January 4, 2022, Albany County DA David Soares declined to prosecute and asked a judge to dismiss the criminal complaint, citing insufficient evidence to meet the burden of proof at trial — even while finding the complainant credible. On January 7, 2022, Judge Holly Drexler officially dismissed the complaint. The remaining four criminal inquiries in other counties were also dismissed; the last was dropped by Oswego County DA on January 31, 2022. Cuomo had resigned as governor in August 2021 amid a separate sexual harassment investigation by the NY AG.
source1_description: New York Attorney General: Statement After Criminal Charges Brought Against Former Governor Cuomo (October 28, 2021)
source2_description: Wikipedia: Andrew Cuomo sexual harassment allegations — Criminal investigation section
notes: Cuomo resigned as governor before formal criminal charges were filed but after the AG's investigative report. The criminal complaint constituted a formal charge under New York law. Cuomo denied all allegations of criminal conduct. As of 2025, a separate DOJ investigation into nursing home COVID-19 deaths was opened.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Eric Greitens
charges_filed: Count 1: Felony invasion of privacy (photographing a woman in a state of nudity without consent, for alleged use as blackmail); Count 2: Felony computer tampering (misappropriating a donor list from his charity, The Mission Continues, for campaign fundraising)
jurisdiction: St. Louis Circuit Court, Missouri
case_number: Not publicly available (St. Louis Circuit Court, 2018)
resolution_details: Count 1 (invasion of privacy) was dropped by St. Louis Circuit Attorney Kimberly Gardner on May 14, 2018, after her investigator was found to have given false testimony. Count 2 (computer tampering) was dropped on May 30, 2018, as part of an agreement tied to Greitens's resignation — Gardner stated prosecutors had sufficient evidence to convict but that pursuing a conviction was not worth the cost to Missouri government after his departure. Greitens resigned effective June 1, 2018.
source1_description: NPR: 1 Charge Against Missouri Gov. Eric Greitens Dropped As He Resigns (May 30, 2018)
source2_description: CNN Politics: Felony charge dropped against Eric Greitens (May 30, 2018)
notes: The prosecution's case was significantly undermined when Gardner's investigator was found to have given false testimony in hearings. Greitens was later elected to the U.S. Senate from Missouri in 2022.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Bob McDonnell
charges_filed: Fourteen counts: conspiracy to commit honest services fraud, honest services fraud (18 U.S.C. §§1343, 1349), conspiracy to commit Hobbs Act extortion, Hobbs Act extortion (18 U.S.C. §1951), and making false statements (18 U.S.C. §1014) — related to $175,000 in gifts and loans from Jonnie Williams (Star Scientific CEO) in exchange for promoting Williams's dietary supplement
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Richmond); U.S. Supreme Court (No. 15-474)
resolution_details: McDonnell was convicted at trial in September 2014 and sentenced to two years in prison; his wife received a year and a day. The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously reversed his conviction on June 27, 2016 (McDonnell v. United States, 579 U.S. 550), holding that jury instructions employed an impermissibly broad definition of 'official act.' The Court ruled that merely arranging meetings or hosting events — without more — could not constitute an official act triggering a corruption conviction. The DOJ announced on September 8, 2016, that it would dismiss all charges with prejudice and not seek a retrial.
source1_description: Supreme Court of the United States: McDonnell v. United States, 579 U.S. ___ (2016) — Justia
source2_description: National Constitution Center: Federal government ends case against McDonnells (September 8, 2016)
notes: Neither McDonnell nor his wife served any prison time due to appellate stays. The Supreme Court's ruling significantly raised the bar for federal public corruption prosecutions nationwide and benefited other public officials including William Jefferson.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: James Traficant
charges_filed: RICO bribery conspiracy (18 U.S.C. §§1961 et seq.) — accepting $163,000 in bribes from organized crime figures (the Carrabia brothers, James Prato, and Joseph Naples Jr.) during and after his 1980 campaign for Mahoning County Sheriff
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Ohio (Eastern Division)
case_number: Indicted August 9, 1982 (N.D. Ohio — case cited as United States v. Traficant, 558 F. Supp. 996)
resolution_details: Traficant represented himself at trial and was acquitted of all charges in spring 1983 — becoming, according to legal reports, the only person ever to win a federal RICO case while representing himself pro se. He argued the payments were actually reverse-sting money used for his own investigation of organized crime. The acquittal elevated his public profile in the Youngstown, Ohio area and launched his political career. He was later elected to the U.S. House of Representatives (1985–2002) before being convicted of separate federal corruption charges in 2002.
source1_description: Justia: United States v. Traficant, 558 F. Supp. 996 (N.D. Ohio 1983) — Court Record
source2_description: Politico: House expels fellow lawmaker (July 24, 2014) — background on 1983 acquittal
notes: This entry covers only the 1983 RICO acquittal. Traficant was later convicted in 2002 on ten separate federal felony counts (racketeering, bribery, tax evasion) as a sitting congressman and was expelled from the House — that later conviction is outside the scope of this database.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Caesar Goodson
charges_filed: Second-degree depraved heart murder; involuntary manslaughter; second-degree assault; manslaughter by vehicle (gross negligence and criminal negligence); misconduct in office — related to the in-custody death of Freddie Gray in April 2015
jurisdiction: Circuit Court of Baltimore City, Maryland
case_number: Not publicly available (Baltimore City Circuit Court, 2015–2016)
resolution_details: Goodson, who drove the police van in which Freddie Gray sustained a fatal spinal cord injury, faced the most severe charges of the six officers. Judge Barry Williams acquitted Goodson of all counts in a bench trial on June 23, 2016, finding that the State had failed to prove criminal intent. Williams ruled that officers had discretion regarding seat-belting and that it was not clear Gray had been injured until near the end of the van ride. After three other bench trials resulted in not-guilty verdicts, State's Attorney Marilyn Mosby dropped all remaining charges against the remaining three officers on July 27, 2016.
source1_description: NPR: Prosecutors Drop All Remaining Charges Against Officers In Freddie Gray's Death (July 27, 2016)
source2_description: NBC News: Caesar Goodson, Baltimore Van Driver, Acquitted of All Charges in Freddie Gray Death (June 23, 2016)
notes: The six officers charged in connection with Freddie Gray's death were: Lt. Brian Rice (acquitted), Officers Caesar Goodson, Edward Nero, and William Porter (mistrial), and Officers Garrett Miller and Sgt. Alicia White (charges dropped). None were convicted. The DOJ also declined to bring federal civil rights charges in September 2017.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Edward Nero
charges_filed: Two counts second-degree assault; manslaughter by vehicle (gross negligence); two counts misconduct in office; false imprisonment — related to the arrest and in-custody death of Freddie Gray
jurisdiction: Circuit Court of Baltimore City, Maryland
case_number: Not publicly available (Baltimore City Circuit Court, 2015–2016)
resolution_details: In a bench trial, Judge Barry Williams acquitted Nero of all charges on May 23, 2016. The judge held that the State failed to prove criminal intent and that Nero's actions in arresting and transporting Gray were not unlawful under the law. This was the second of the six officers to go to trial and resulted in not-guilty verdicts across all counts.
source1_description: NPR: Prosecutors Drop All Remaining Charges Against Officers In Freddie Gray's Death (July 27, 2016)
source2_description: New York Times: Charges Dropped Against 3 Remaining Officers in Freddie Gray Case (July 28, 2016)
notes: Officer William Porter, whose trial was the first and ended in a hung jury and mistrial in December 2015, had remaining charges dropped on July 27, 2016 along with Garrett Miller and Sgt. Alicia White, before their cases went to trial.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Brian Rice
charges_filed: Involuntary manslaughter; two counts second-degree assault; manslaughter by vehicle (gross negligence); two counts misconduct in office; false imprisonment — related to the arrest and in-custody death of Freddie Gray
jurisdiction: Circuit Court of Baltimore City, Maryland
case_number: Not publicly available (Baltimore City Circuit Court, 2015–2016)
resolution_details: Rice, the highest-ranking of the six officers charged, was acquitted of all charges (manslaughter, reckless endangerment, and misconduct in office) in a bench trial by Judge Barry Williams on July 18, 2016. Following this fourth non-conviction result, State's Attorney Mosby dropped the remaining charges against Officers Miller and White and retrial charges against Porter on July 27, 2016.
source1_description: NPR: Prosecutors Drop All Remaining Charges Against Officers In Freddie Gray's Death (July 27, 2016)
source2_description: Fox 5 DC: Baltimore Police Lt. Brian Rice acquitted of all charges in Freddie Gray case (July 18, 2016)
notes: Rice was the highest-ranking officer and the last to be acquitted before the remaining prosecutions were abandoned. Mosby's decision to drop all remaining charges was described as 'agonizing.'
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Jeronimo Yanez
role_title: Police Officer, St. Anthony Police Department (MN)
charges_filed: Second-degree manslaughter; two counts of dangerous discharge of a firearm — for the July 6, 2016, fatal shooting of Philando Castile during a traffic stop in Falcon Heights, Minnesota
jurisdiction: Ramsey County District Court, Minnesota
resolution_details: After five days of deliberation and over 25 hours of deliberation, a 12-member jury acquitted Yanez of all charges on June 16, 2017. The jury was initially split 10-2 in favor of acquittal; the two holdouts were ultimately persuaded. A juror who spoke anonymously afterward stated that the specific statutory wording on culpable negligence was the deciding factor. Yanez was immediately fired by the City of St. Anthony following the verdict. The city of St. Anthony paid a $3.8 million civil settlement to Castile's family.
source1_description: NPR: Jury Acquits Minnesota Officer In Shooting Death Of Philando Castile (June 16, 2017)
source2_description: Wikipedia: Killing of Philando Castile — Trial and acquittal
notes: The shooting was live-streamed on Facebook by Castile's girlfriend, Diamond Reynolds, and was widely seen nationally. Castile, a Black man, had informed Yanez he was a licensed gun carrier before reaching for his identification. The acquittal sparked significant protests across Minnesota.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Thomas Gaffney
charges_filed: Conspiracy; official misconduct; obstruction of justice — for allegedly filing false police reports to conceal the true circumstances of Officer Jason Van Dyke's fatal shooting of 17-year-old Laquan McDonald on October 20, 2014
jurisdiction: Cook County Circuit Court, Illinois
case_number: Not publicly available (Cook County Circuit Court, 2018–2019)
resolution_details: Gaffney, along with former Officer Joseph Walsh and former Detective David March, waived their right to a jury trial. Cook County Associate Judge Domenica Stephenson acquitted all three on January 17, 2019, after finding that the State had failed to prove beyond a reasonable doubt that any conspiracy existed or that the officers had lied in any official capacity. The judge stated: 'The evidence shows just the opposite.' She found that two people witnessing the same incident could recount it differently without that constituting evidence of deceit.
source1_description: PBS News: Chicago police officers acquitted of covering up Laquan McDonald shooting (January 17, 2019)
source2_description: New York Times: 3 Officers Acquitted of Covering Up for Colleague in Laquan McDonald Shooting (January 17, 2019)
notes: This was reportedly the first time in Chicago history that police officers faced criminal charges stemming from an on-duty shooting. Jason Van Dyke (the officer who fired 16 shots at McDonald) was convicted of second-degree murder in October 2018 — that conviction stands and is outside this database's scope. Former Officer Joseph Walsh and former Detective David March were acquitted alongside Gaffney.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Joseph Walsh
charges_filed: Conspiracy; official misconduct; obstruction of justice — for allegedly filing false police reports and conspiring to conceal the facts of Officer Jason Van Dyke's fatal shooting of Laquan McDonald on October 20, 2014
jurisdiction: Cook County Circuit Court, Illinois
case_number: Not publicly available (Cook County Circuit Court, 2018–2019)
resolution_details: Acquitted in the same bench trial as Thomas Gaffney and David March on January 17, 2019. Judge Stephenson found the prosecution's evidence — including police reports, dashcam video, and witness testimony — insufficient to establish a conspiracy or deliberate lying. Walsh had been present at the scene when Van Dyke shot McDonald.
source1_description: WTTW News: Chicago Cops Found Not Guilty in Laquan McDonald Cover-Up Trial (January 17, 2019)
source2_description: NPR/WBEZ: Chicago Cops Found Not Guilty Of Cover-Up In Laquan McDonald Shooting Case (January 17, 2019)
notes: Walsh was among the officers who had filed police reports describing McDonald as the aggressor in a manner that prosecutors contended was inconsistent with the dashcam video. The judge's acquittal was described as 'stunning' by the Chicago Tribune.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: David March
charges_filed: Conspiracy; official misconduct; obstruction of justice — for allegedly using his role as the lead detective investigating the Laquan McDonald shooting to shape a false narrative justifying Van Dyke's use of force
jurisdiction: Cook County Circuit Court, Illinois
case_number: Not publicly available (Cook County Circuit Court, 2018–2019)
resolution_details: Acquitted in the same bench trial as Gaffney and Walsh on January 17, 2019. March was not present at the scene but was the detective assigned to investigate the shooting. Prosecutors alleged he used his investigative role to bury evidence and construct a false justification for the shooting. The judge found no evidence of a coordinated conspiracy and that evidence of lying had not been proven beyond a reasonable doubt.
source1_description: WTTW News: Chicago Cops Found Not Guilty in Laquan McDonald Cover-Up Trial (January 17, 2019)
source2_description: BBC News: Laquan McDonald: Chicago officers cleared of shooting cover-up (January 17, 2019)
notes: March's unique position — as an investigative detective rather than a scene participant — made his case legally distinct. The prosecution relied heavily on what they alleged were false investigative reports filed by March to justify Van Dyke's actions.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Joshua Jaynes
charges_filed: Aiding and abetting deprivation of civil rights (18 U.S.C. §242) — Fourth Amendment violation; conspiracy to falsify records and obstruct justice (18 U.S.C. §§1512, 1519, 371) — for allegedly drafting a false search warrant affidavit that led to the March 2020 raid on Breonna Taylor's apartment and her death
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Kentucky
resolution_details: Jaynes was originally charged in August 2022 with felony civil rights violations. On two separate occasions (2023 and 2025), the district court judge reduced felony charges to misdemeanors, ruling the government could not attribute Taylor's death to the defective warrant under applicable law. On March 21, 2026, the DOJ (under Trump administration, signed by AG Harmeet Dhillon) moved to dismiss all remaining charges with prejudice, citing the failed felony enhancement attempts and a review 'in the interest of justice.' The case was pending final court approval at time of research.
source1_description: CBS News: DOJ moves to dismiss criminal case against ex-police officers charged for role in Breonna Taylor's death (March 21, 2026)
source2_description: WDRB News: DOJ moves to dismiss criminal charges against 2 former LMPD officers in Breonna Taylor case (March 21, 2026)
notes: Taylor, 26, was killed when police executed the warrant at her apartment in March 2020, finding no drugs or cash. Jaynes allegedly drafted a warrant affidavit containing false statements about drug dealer Jamarcus Glover receiving packages at Taylor's address. Taylor's family expressed deep disappointment at the dismissals. Kyle Meany (see separate entry) was co-defendant in the same case.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Kyle Meany
charges_filed: Aiding and abetting deprivation of civil rights (18 U.S.C. §242) — Fourth Amendment violation; making false statements to the FBI (18 U.S.C. §1001) — for allegedly approving a false search warrant affidavit and lying to federal investigators during the Breonna Taylor case
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Kentucky
resolution_details: Co-defendant with Joshua Jaynes in the same federal case. Meany was accused of signing off on the false warrant affidavit and lying to the FBI by falsely claiming LMPD's SWAT unit requested the no-knock warrant authority. Like Jaynes, the court reduced felony civil rights charges to misdemeanors in 2024. The DOJ moved to dismiss all remaining charges with prejudice on March 21, 2026, citing the two failed felony enhancement rulings and a review in the interest of justice.
source1_description: CBS News: DOJ moves to dismiss criminal case against ex-police officers charged for role in Breonna Taylor's death (March 21, 2026)
source2_description: BBC News: Justice department seeks to dismiss charges against ex-officers over Breonna Taylor raid (March 21, 2026)
notes: Note: Brett Hankison, the only other LMPD officer criminally charged in connection with Taylor's death, was convicted of federal civil rights violations in November 2024 (not included in this database). Meany's attorney called the DOJ's dismissal motion result 'extreme thankfulness.' Taylor's mother described the dismissals as 'deeply painful.'
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Brett Hankison
charges_filed: State (Kentucky): Three counts of wanton endangerment (1st degree) — firing bullets through Taylor's window and sliding door into a neighboring apartment during the 2020 raid
jurisdiction: Jefferson Circuit Court, Kentucky (state trial)
resolution_details: Hankison was acquitted of all three state wanton endangerment counts by a jury on March 3, 2022. The charges related to putting Taylor's neighbors at risk — not Taylor herself, as no officer was charged with her death. Hankison fired 10 shots through covered windows and a sliding door into an adjacent apartment where three people were present; none were struck. The jury found the state had not met its burden. NOTE: Hankison was subsequently tried in federal court; a 2023 federal trial ended in a mistrial, and a 2024 federal retrial resulted in conviction for violating Taylor's civil rights. This entry covers only the state acquittal.
source1_description: ABC News: Jury finds ex-officer Brett Hankison not guilty on all counts in shooting during Breonna Taylor raid (March 4, 2022)
source2_description: New York Times: Officer Acquitted of Endangering Breonna Taylor's Neighbors in Raid (March 3, 2022)
notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: This entry covers ONLY the 2022 Kentucky state acquittal. Hankison was later convicted in federal court in November 2024 on a civil rights charge related to Breonna Taylor and sentenced to 33 months (July 2025). His federal conviction is documented separately. Including here only due to the state-level acquittal.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED (STATE CHARGES). Kentucky state charges resulted in acquittal. Note: subsequent federal conviction obtained in 2024.

name: Henry Cisneros
role_title: Secretary of Housing and Urban Development (HUD)
government_body: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development — Clinton Administration Cabinet
charges_filed: 18 counts: conspiracy (Count 1) to withhold information from the FBI; making false statements to the FBI background check (Counts 2–19); obstructing a DOJ security clearance determination (Count 18) — for lying about payments made to former mistress Linda Medlar during his HUD confirmation process
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia
case_number: United States v. Cisneros, 26 F. Supp. 2d 24 (D.D.C. 1998)
resolution_details: Cisneros was indicted in 1995 on 18 counts but ultimately pleaded guilty in 1997 to a single misdemeanor — making a false statement (18 U.S.C. §1001) — pursuant to a plea agreement in which the remaining 17 felony counts were dismissed. He was sentenced to a $10,000 fine and no prison time. On January 19, 2001, outgoing President Bill Clinton granted Cisneros a full pardon, expunging even the misdemeanor conviction. Independent Counsel David Barrett's 10-year, $21 million investigation ended without any charges beyond the initial plea. The final IC report, released in 2006, accused the Clinton administration of impeding the inquiry.
source1_description: Justia: United States v. Cisneros, 26 F. Supp. 2d 24 (D.D.C. 1998) — Case Record
source2_description: NPR: Probe into Ex-Clinton Official Ends with No Charges (January 19, 2006)
notes: IMPORTANT CAVEAT: Cisneros did plead guilty to a misdemeanor in 1997 (making a false statement). However, because he was subsequently pardoned by President Clinton in 2001, no conviction stands. The 17 felony counts were never prosecuted. This entry is included because — like Stevens and DeLay — no final criminal conviction remains. The pardon/plea resolution is noted prominently.
disclaimer: NOTE: Pleaded guilty to one misdemeanor count in 1997; received a full presidential pardon in 2001. The 17 felony counts were dismissed. No conviction currently stands.

name: William Porter
charges_filed: Involuntary manslaughter; second-degree assault; misconduct in office — for failing to call for medical attention for Freddie Gray while transporting him in the police van
jurisdiction: Circuit Court of Baltimore City, Maryland
case_number: Not publicly available (Baltimore City Circuit Court, 2015–2016)
resolution_details: Porter's trial was the first of the six Freddie Gray officers to proceed. The jury deadlocked on all four charges on December 16, 2015, resulting in a mistrial. A retrial was scheduled but never completed — on July 27, 2016, following the acquittals of three other officers, State's Attorney Marilyn Mosby dropped all remaining charges against Porter along with Officers Garrett Miller and Sgt. Alicia White. The State cited the pattern of acquittals and the systemic challenges of prosecuting police officers as reasons for abandoning the remaining cases.
source1_description: NPR: Prosecutors Drop All Remaining Charges Against Officers In Freddie Gray's Death (July 27, 2016)
source2_description: New York Times: Charges Dropped Against 3 Remaining Officers in Freddie Gray Case (July 28, 2016)
notes: Porter was set to be called as a witness in the cases of other officers, which created legal complications regarding his Fifth Amendment rights. The circuit court ruled in December 2015 that Porter could be compelled to testify with use immunity. His retrial was repeatedly delayed by these complications before Mosby dropped all charges.
disclaimer: CHARGED ONLY — NOT CONVICTED. Charges were filed but did not result in a conviction.

name: Paul Campo
role_title: Former Deputy Chief, DEA Office of Financial Operations; 25-year DEA veteran
charges_filed: Narco-terrorism conspiracy, cocaine trafficking conspiracy, providing material support to a foreign terrorist organization (CJNG — Jalisco New Generation Cartel), money laundering conspiracy — allegedly agreed to launder $12 million for what he believed was CJNG; caught in FBI/DEA sting (pleaded not guilty; detained without bail)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, S.D. New York (federal)
resolution: CHARGED NOT CONVICTED — Pleaded not guilty; trial pending
resolution_details: Pleaded not guilty; trial pending
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Campo indictment
source2_description: New York Times coverage
disclaimer: ⚠ WARNING: This individual has been charged but NOT convicted. Under U.S. law, defendants are presumed innocent until proven guilty beyond a reasonable doubt.
